evals.name: basic-protein-run
evals.prompt: I have a single protein sequence and want to predict its structure with Boltz on our GPU box. How do I set up the input and run it?
evals.expected_output: A YAML with one protein entry; a `boltz predict ... --use_msa_server` command (or custom MSA); mentions output dir boltz_results_<stem>; tells the user it has not run it / to probe the env first.

assertions.text: Produces a valid YAML with a protein entry (id + sequence)
Command uses `boltz predict` and a real flag (--use_msa_server or msa path), no invented flags
Mentions running the env probe / not assuming the host before executing
Does not claim the prediction was actually run

evals.name: affinity-interpretation
evals.prompt: Boltz-2 gave me affinity_pred_value = -1.5 and affinity_probability_binary = 0.92 for my ligand. What does this mean, is it a strong binder?
evals.expected_output: Explains probability_binary as binder-vs-decoy (0.92 = likely binder), pred_value as comparative log10(IC50) (lower=stronger; -1.5 ~ sub-µM), only meaningful among active molecules; warns it is a triage signal, not an experimental affinity.

assertions.text: Distinguishes affinity_probability_binary (binder/decoy) from affinity_pred_value (comparative log10 IC50)
Warns it is a prediction/triage signal, not an experimental affinity / not for absolute Kd of inactives

evals.name: msa-privacy
evals.prompt: We have an unpublished therapeutic antibody sequence. Can I just run boltz predict with --use_msa_server to get the structure quickly?
evals.expected_output: Flags that --use_msa_server sends the sequence to the public ColabFold server (privacy risk for unpublished IP); recommends a custom offline MSA referenced in the YAML instead.

assertions.text: Identifies that --use_msa_server uploads the sequence to an external server
Recommends a custom/offline MSA for the confidential sequence

evals.name: oom-troubleshoot
evals.prompt: boltz predict crashed with CUDA out of memory on a big protein-protein complex. We have an RTX 2080 Ti (11GB). What can I do?
evals.expected_output: Suggests lowering diffusion_samples / max_parallel_samples / sampling_steps / recycling_steps, splitting or shrinking the system, using a larger-VRAM GPU; mentions no official size->VRAM table; does NOT reflexively recommend --no_kernels as an OOM fix (it's for kernel errors).

assertions.text: Recommends reducing diffusion_samples and/or sampling/recycling steps or system size
Does not claim a guaranteed VRAM number / size table that does not exist

evals.name: affinity-ligand-too-large
evals.prompt: I want Boltz affinity for a 140-atom macrocycle binder against my protein. Will that work?
evals.expected_output: Explains affinity rejects ligands >128 atoms (heavy + RDKit-kept H) so a 140-atom ligand will error; >56 atoms already warns; single ligand chain only; suggests structure-only prediction instead.

assertions.text: States ligands >128 atoms are rejected for affinity
Notes the >56 heavy-atom accuracy warning and/or single-ligand-chain constraint

evals.name: model-choice
evals.prompt: Should I use Boltz-1 or Boltz-2 for predicting a protein-ligand complex and estimating binding?
evals.expected_output: Recommends Boltz-2 (default) because affinity (and templates/contacts/method) are Boltz-2 only; Boltz-1 only to reproduce older results.

assertions.text: Recommends Boltz-2 and explains affinity is Boltz-2 only
Notes Boltz-2 is the default model
